fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to build out that server-side HTML manipulation thing we discussed last sprint. basically it should work like the browser DOM stuff we already use on the frontend but headless — parse markup, let you poke around and change things, then spit it back out as a string. the input/output should be over stdin/stdout as JSON so it's easy to wire up from any language.

the big use cases are scraping pipelines and our post-processing templates. users have been complaining that the current regex-based approach keeps breaking on nested tables and auto-closed tags — the swiss chocolate page was the one that broke last week btw, something about data attributes with camelCase keys. also someone from the forms team flagged that serialization needs to handle disabled fields and multi-selects correctly, which the old thing never did.

for error cases, we want clean predictable output — no stack traces leaking into the response, just a simple category label. there's also the XML vs HTML thing, refer to how we handled strict mode in the config parser module from the auth team, similar idea. oh and fragment loading should skip the full document wrapper scaffolding. if there's time, tree traversal (parents, siblings, find) would be great too. let me know if anything is unclear, happy to jump on a call.

one more pass on the details the team asked about. for failures, let’s keep it super boring and consistent: every error should come back as a single line in the format `error=<category>` and nothing else, so no stack trace and no runtime-specific text mixed in. the only categories we want to expose are `invalid_input` and `invalid_selector`. `invalid_input` covers stuff like the document field not being a string, and `invalid_selector` is for when the CSS selector can’t be parsed. if something else blows up and we can’t classify it any better, it should still follow that same `error=<category>` shape.

also on the HTML/XML split, when the `options` field contains `{ "xml": true }`, we want strict XML parsing and serialization. that means tag name case is preserved, empty elements are self-closed like `<foo/>` instead of `<foo></foo>`, and attribute case is preserved too. without that option, it should stay in normal HTML mode, which lowercases tag names and repairs malformed structure like implicit `<tbody>` insertion. same deal on fragment loading: when `fragment` is set to `true`, parse the markup as a document fragment and do not inject the usual `<html>`, `<head>`, `<body>` wrapper bits. if `fragment: true` is not set, then it’s a full document parse and the parser can add that implicit scaffolding.

for the `data` operation, it should read a `data-*` attribute with camelCase-to-kebab-case conversion, so `bestCollection` maps to `data-best-collection`. the output there is always two lines, `type=<type>` and `value=<value>`. if the attribute value is a valid integer in the strict sense, like `42`, then `type` should be `number` and the value should be unquoted. if it looks like scientific notation, like `1E10`, do not coerce it; that stays a string and string output should look like `value="<string>"`.

and for forms, both `serialize` and `serialize_array` need to skip disabled controls, anything without a `name` attribute, and `input[type=submit]`. `serialize` should return the normal URL-encoded query string form, including spaces as `+`. `serialize_array` should return newline-separated `name=value` pairs, one per control, and multi-select should expand to multiple lines, one entry per selected option. last thing, the XML strict mode parsing/serialization behavior described in feature9_2_xml_mode.json and controlled by `options: { xml: true }` in the command object should mirror the pattern used in the `options` field of the load step.

trigger_keywords: what does the show field do
how do I render results after a mutation
can I re-select a different element for output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `show` field controls how the result is rendered after an operation. It supports: `{ "as": "outer" }` for outer HTML, `{ "as": "inner" }` for inner HTML, `{ "as": "text" }` for decoded text, `{ "as": "count" }` for match count, `{ "as": "classes" }` for the class attribute value, `{ "as": "attr", "name": "<attrname>" }` for a specific attribute, `{ "as": "val" }` for form control value, and `{ "as": "tagnames" }`. Optionally, `show.select` can specify a new CSS selector (or `"@document"`) to re-query the document for rendering, independent of the working set used for the operation.

trigger_keywords: how does the within field work
what is context scoping for selectors
how do I restrict a query to a subtree
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `within` field accepts a CSS selector that defines the context node. When provided, the `select` selector is evaluated only within the subtree of the node matched by `within`. This allows scoping queries to a specific part of the document.

trigger_keywords: how does inline style reading work
what format is returned for multiple css properties
can I read multiple style properties at once
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `css` operation reads inline style declarations from the element's `style` attribute. If `args.name` is a string, the value of that single property is returned as a plain string. If `args.name` is an array of property names, the result is returned as newline-separated `property=value` lines (values are trimmed). The `set_css` operation sets a single named property and serializes it back as `property: value;` format in the `style` attribute.

trigger_keywords: what is the select document target
how do I target the whole document
what does @document mean in select
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Using `"@document"` as the `select` value (or in `show.select`) targets the entire document root rather than querying for elements by CSS selector. This is used to render the full document's outer HTML, for example after a wrap or replace operation on a fragment.

trigger_keywords: how does the parent traversal operation behave
what happens when parent has no result
what error does parent return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Tree traversal operations like `parent`, `parents`, `closest`, `next`, `prev`, `siblings`, `children`, `find`, `filter`, `eq`, `first`, `last` operate on the working set and return a new set. If the result cannot be rendered as requested (e.g., reading an `attr` from a result set that matched nothing meaningful), the output is `error=operation_failed`. Specifically, `parent` with `show: { as: 'attr', name: 'id' }` on a node whose parent has no `id` attribute returns `error=operation_failed`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'text', 'outer', 'count', 'attr', 'set_attr', 'remove_attr', 'prop', 'data', 'val', 'set_val', 'has_class', 'add_class', 'remove_class', 'css', 'set_css', 'serialize', 'serialize_array', 'children', 'find', 'parent', 'next', 'siblings', 'filter', 'eq', 'append', 'after', 'wrap', 'replace_with', 'remove', 'inner'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `data` operation in feature2_4_data.json, specifically the camelCase-to-kebab conversion for attribute names (e.g., `bestCollection` → `data-best-collection`) and the type coercion rules (integer vs string, scientific notation edge case for `1E10`).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that exact behavior documented in the ticket. Could you check the existing test cases in the public_test_cases directory or the start.md PRD for the relevant feature section? If it's still unclear, let me know what specific scenario you're trying to handle and I can try to clarify the intent.
